name: राजविद्याराजगुह्ययोग
teaching: The secret hidden in plain sight: everything rests in the divine the way wind rests in space — held, pervaded, yet never grasping or grasped. No elaborate rite is needed; a leaf, a flower, water, offered with love, is received. And the door is closed to no one — whoever comes, however late, however fallen, arrives. The sovereign secret is intimacy: the universe rests in Him who is not contained by it, the smallest leaf offered in love is accepted, and no past, however broken, disqualifies refuge. He carries what His lovers lack.
questions: What is the smallest thing you could offer today — and could you actually offer it?
Krishna says he is the same to all beings. Where do you assume you are disqualified?
What would change if you believed nothing devotional is ever lost?

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
इदं तु ते गुह्यतमं प्रवक्ष्याम्यनसूयवे ।
ज्ञानं विज्ञानसहितं यज्ज्ञात्वा मोक्ष्यसेऽशुभात्॥
verses.transliteration:
idaṃ tu te guhyatamaṃ pravakṣyāmyanasūyave
jñānaṃ vijñānasahitaṃ yajjñātvā mokṣyase'śubhāt
verses.essence: To you, who do not cavil, I shall declare this most secret knowledge together with realization — knowing which you will be freed from misfortune.
verses.translation: I shall now declare to thee who does not cavil, the greatest secret, the knowledge combined with experience (Self-realisation). Having known this thou shalt be free evil.

anvaya.meaning: but this, to you, most secret
I shall declare — to the uncavilling
knowledge together with realization
knowing which you will be released from ill

verses.explanation: Guhya-tamam — not just secret but the superlative, the innermost chamber. And the entry qualification is named again: anasūyave, to one free of the carping spirit. Secrets of this order are not hidden by locks but by attitude; cynicism cannot hear them even when they are spoken aloud.
verses.connection: Opens the chapter Krishna will call the king of teachings — to the one listener fit to receive it.
verses.reflection: Receive one teaching today the way this chapter is given: as a secret entrusted, not data downloaded.
verses.background: Krishna leans closer, as it were, mid-battlefield: to you who do not cavil, he says, I now declare the most secret knowledge — the royal science, the royal mystery, directly experienceable, righteous, joyful to practice, imperishable. Only the faithless miss it and return to the mortal round.

verses.devanagari:
राजविद्या राजगुह्यं पवित्रमिदमुत्तमम् ।
प्रत्यक्षावगमं धर्म्यं सुसुखं कर्तुमव्ययम्॥
verses.transliteration:
rājavidyā rājaguhyaṃ pavitramidamuttamam
pratyakṣāvagamaṃ dharmyaṃ susukhaṃ kartumavyayam
verses.essence: This is the king of knowledge, the king of secrets, the supreme purifier — known by direct experience, in accord with dharma, easy to practice, imperishable.
verses.translation: This is the kingl science, the kingly secret, the supreme purifier, realisable by direct intuitional knowledge, according to righteousness, very easy to perform and imperishable.

anvaya.meaning: king of sciences, king of secrets
this supreme purifier
known by direct perception, lawful
very easy to do, imperishable

verses.explanation: Six titles, two of them startling: pratyakṣāvagamam — verifiable in one's own experience, not on hearsay — and su-sukham kartum, *easy* and *pleasant* to practice. The supreme secret is not an ordeal but the one discipline that delights as it works. Its only real obstacle, the chapter implies, is disbelief that it could be this direct.
verses.connection: The credentials of the royal teaching — culminating in the chapter's heart, devotion.
verses.reflection: Test the royal claim yourself: this knowing is verifiable here, pleasant to practice, imperishable. Verify.
verses.background: Krishna leans closer, as it were, mid-battlefield: to you who do not cavil, he says, I now declare the most secret knowledge — the royal science, the royal mystery, directly experienceable, righteous, joyful to practice, imperishable. Only the faithless miss it and return to the mortal round.

verses.devanagari:
अश्रद्दधानाः पुरुषा धर्मस्यास्य परन्तप ।
अप्राप्य मां निवर्तन्ते मृत्युसंसारवर्त्मनि॥
verses.transliteration:
aśraddadhānāḥ puruṣā dharmasyāsya parantapa
aprāpya māṃ nivartante mṛtyusaṃsāravartmani
verses.essence: People without faith in this dharma, Parantapa, fail to reach me and return to the round of death and rebirth.
verses.translation: Those who have no faith in this Dharma (knowledge of the Self), O Parantapa (Arjuna), return to the path of this world of death without attaining Me.

anvaya.meaning: people without faith
in this dharma, O Parantapa
not attaining me, they return
on the path of death-samsara

verses.explanation: The single failure mode of an easy path: aśraddhā, the refusal to trust it enough to try. No punishment is decreed — only the default: nivartante, they go on circling, because the exit, though open, was never believed in. Faith here is not credulity but the minimal trust any door requires before it can be walked through.
verses.connection: The one fine-print clause on 9.2's accessibility: ease helps only those who believe in the door.
verses.reflection: Notice the one entrance fee named: faith. Bring some to one practice today and watch the door move.
verses.background: Krishna leans closer, as it were, mid-battlefield: to you who do not cavil, he says, I now declare the most secret knowledge — the royal science, the royal mystery, directly experienceable, righteous, joyful to practice, imperishable. Only the faithless miss it and return to the mortal round.

verses.devanagari:
मया ततमिदं सर्वं जगदव्यक्तमूर्तिना ।
मत्स्थानि सर्वभूतानि न चाहं तेष्ववस्थितः॥
verses.transliteration:
mayā tatamidaṃ sarvaṃ jagadavyaktamūrtinā
matsthāni sarvabhūtāni na cāhaṃ teṣvavasthitaḥ
verses.essence: By me, in my unmanifest form, all this universe is pervaded. All beings rest in me; I do not rest in them.
verses.translation: All this world is pervaded by Me in My unmanifest aspect; all beings exist in Me, but I do not dwell in them.

anvaya.meaning: by me is pervaded all this
universe, in unmanifest form
all beings abide in me
and I am not situated in them

verses.explanation: The royal secret begins as paradox: everything rests in him, he rests in nothing — the way all waves are in the ocean while the ocean is not 'in' any wave. Pervasion without containment: he is the ground of each thing without being a tenant of any thing. Hold the discomfort; the next verse will deepen it on purpose. Maya tatam idam sarvam — all this is pervaded by Me in unmanifest form; all beings rest in Me — na chaham teshv avasthitah, and I am not in them. The next verse will even revoke the resting. The Gaudiya school built its central doctrine here: achintya-bhedabheda, inconceivable difference-and-nondifference — the verse held as proof that the God-world relation breaks every container, including 'containment' itself.
verses.connection: First half of the chapter's great paradox — to be deliberately overturned in 9.5.
verses.schools_note: 'All beings rest in Me — and I am not in them,' revoked again in 9.5 ('nor do beings rest in Me'): the deliberate contradiction is each school's Rorschach. Shankara: the relation is illusory from the absolute side. Ramanuja: pervasion without dependence — the world is His body, He untouched by its modes. The Gaudiyas coined their school's name from it: achintya-bhedabheda, inconceivable simultaneous difference and non-difference.
verses.reflection: Feel once today: everything around you is in Him — and He stands free of all of it.
verses.background: The sovereign paradox is stated: all this world is pervaded by Me; all beings rest in Me — and I am not in them; nor do beings rest in Me. Behold My divine yoga: like wind standing in space, beings stand in Me; nature, under My supervision, produces the moving and unmoving.

verses.devanagari:
न च मत्स्थानि भूतानि पश्य मे योगमैश्वरम् ।
भूतभृन्न च भूतस्थो ममात्मा भूतभावनः॥
verses.transliteration:
na ca matsthāni bhūtāni paśya me yogamaiśvaram
bhūtabhṛnna ca bhūtastho mamātmā bhūtabhāvanaḥ
verses.essence: And yet beings do not rest in me — behold my divine yoga! Sustaining beings, yet not dwelling in them, my Self is the very source of beings.
verses.translation: Nor do beings exist in Me (in reality); behold My divine Yoga, supporting all beings, but not dwelling in them, is My Self, the efficient cause of beings.

anvaya.meaning: and (yet) beings do not abide in me
behold my lordly yoga
bearer of beings, yet not abiding in beings
my Self, the source of beings

verses.explanation: The previous verse revoked in the same breath it was confirmed: beings are in me — and beings are *not* in me. Paśya me yogam aiśvaram: this is not contradiction but the signature of a relation no spatial language can hold; 'in' and 'not in' both fail upward. God's connection to the world is realer than containment — which is why containment-talk must break.
verses.connection: Deliberately fractures 9.4's formula: the truth lives in the tension, not in either half.
verses.reflection: Hold the correction too: not even contained — sovereign. Let God outgrow your best container today.
verses.background: The sovereign paradox is stated: all this world is pervaded by Me; all beings rest in Me — and I am not in them; nor do beings rest in Me. Behold My divine yoga: like wind standing in space, beings stand in Me; nature, under My supervision, produces the moving and unmoving.

verses.devanagari:
यथाकाशस्थितो नित्यं वायुः सर्वत्रगो महान् ।
तथा सर्वाणि भूतानि मत्स्थानीत्युपधारय॥
verses.transliteration:
yathākāśasthito nityaṃ vāyuḥ sarvatrago mahān
tathā sarvāṇi bhūtāni matsthānītyupadhāraya
verses.essence: As the mighty wind, moving everywhere, rests always in space — so, understand, do all beings rest in me.
verses.translation: As the mighty wind, moving everywhere, rests always in the ether, even so, know thou that all beings rest in Me.

anvaya.meaning: as, abiding in space, always
the great wind, going everywhere
so all beings
abide in me — thus understand

verses.explanation: The healing image after the fracture: wind in space. Space holds every gust without being pushed by any; the wind moves wildly, the sky it moves through is unmoved, untouched, yet never absent from a single eddy. So beings in him: fully held, never gripping; the holder unstained by the held.
verses.connection: Resolves the 9.4–5 paradox in one simile — intimacy without entanglement.
verses.reflection: Watch wind move through space, owning nothing of it — beings move through Him so. Move so today.
verses.background: The sovereign paradox is stated: all this world is pervaded by Me; all beings rest in Me — and I am not in them; nor do beings rest in Me. Behold My divine yoga: like wind standing in space, beings stand in Me; nature, under My supervision, produces the moving and unmoving.

verses.devanagari:
सर्वभूतानि कौन्तेय प्रकृतिं यान्ति मामिकाम् ।
कल्पक्षये पुनस्तानि कल्पादौ विसृजाम्यहम्॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvabhūtāni kaunteya prakṛtiṃ yānti māmikām
kalpakṣaye punastāni kalpādau visṛjāmyaham
verses.essence: All beings, Kaunteya, enter my nature at the end of a cosmic cycle; at the beginning of the next cycle, I send them forth again.
verses.translation: All beings, O Arjuna, go into My Nature at the end of a Kalpa; I send them forth again at the beginning of (the next) Kalpa.

anvaya.meaning: all beings, O Kaunteya
go to my prakriti
at the kalpa's end; again them
at the kalpa's start I send forth

verses.explanation: The breathing cosmos of chapter 8, now in the first person: at the great evening, beings fold into *my* nature; at the great morning, *I* release them. The cycle has acquired an owner and a will. What looked like impersonal tide from outside is, from his side, deliberate exhalation and welcome home.
verses.connection: Re-states 8.18–19's dissolution-and-emergence with the pronoun that changes its meaning.
verses.reflection: Let one ending today be an in-breath, not a verdict — all beings return to be breathed out again.
verses.background: The sovereign paradox is stated: all this world is pervaded by Me; all beings rest in Me — and I am not in them; nor do beings rest in Me. Behold My divine yoga: like wind standing in space, beings stand in Me; nature, under My supervision, produces the moving and unmoving.

verses.devanagari:
प्रकृतिं स्वामवष्टभ्य विसृजामि पुनः पुनः ।
भूतग्राममिमं कृत्स्नमवशं प्रकृतेर्वशात्॥
verses.transliteration:
prakṛtiṃ svāmavaṣṭabhya visṛjāmi punaḥ punaḥ
bhūtagrāmamimaṃ kṛtsnamavaśaṃ prakṛtervaśāt
verses.essence: Resting on my own nature, I send forth again and again this whole host of beings — helpless under nature's sway.
verses.translation: Animating My Nature, I again and again send forth all this multitude of beings, helpless by the force of the Nature.

anvaya.meaning: leaning upon my own prakriti
I send forth again and again
this entire host of beings
helpless, under prakriti's control

verses.explanation: The mechanics of the great morning: he leans on his nature (avaṣṭabhya — as a king leans on an instrument) and the multitude streams out, avaśam, not by their choice but by the momentum of their own unfinished karma. Creation is thus both his act and their necessity: he opens the door; their unspent tendencies walk them through it.
verses.connection: Details 9.7's sending-forth — and quietly explains why beings return at all: unfinished business.
verses.reflection: Feel nature's helpless reissuing in your own habits — and the freedom of the one presiding over them.
verses.background: The sovereign paradox is stated: all this world is pervaded by Me; all beings rest in Me — and I am not in them; nor do beings rest in Me. Behold My divine yoga: like wind standing in space, beings stand in Me; nature, under My supervision, produces the moving and unmoving.

verses.devanagari:
न च मां तानि कर्माणि निबध्नन्ति धनञ्जय ।
उदासीनवदासीनमसक्तं तेषु कर्मसु॥
verses.transliteration:
na ca māṃ tāni karmāṇi nibadhnanti dhanañjaya
udāsīnavadāsīnamasaktaṃ teṣu karmasu
verses.essence: Nor do these actions bind me, Dhananjaya — seated as one indifferent, unattached to those acts.
verses.translation: These acts do not bind Me, O Arjuna, sitting like one indifferent, unattached to those acts.

anvaya.meaning: nor do those actions me
bind, O Dhananjaya
seated as if indifferent
unattached in those actions

verses.explanation: The Creator audits his own karma and finds none accruing: udāsīna-vat, *as if* uninvolved — the work happens, vast beyond counting, with no craving in the worker. This is chapter 4's secret (action without bondage) demonstrated at maximum scale: if universes can be issued without attachment, an arrow can be loosed without it.
verses.connection: The divine proof-of-concept for everything taught to Arjuna about unbinding action.
verses.reflection: Do one task today seated as if indifferent — fully acting, unattached, the way worlds are made.
verses.background: The sovereign paradox is stated: all this world is pervaded by Me; all beings rest in Me — and I am not in them; nor do beings rest in Me. Behold My divine yoga: like wind standing in space, beings stand in Me; nature, under My supervision, produces the moving and unmoving.

verses.devanagari:
मयाध्यक्षेण प्रकृतिः सूयते सचराचरम् ।
हेतुनानेन कौन्तेय जगद्विपरिवर्तते॥
verses.transliteration:
mayādhyakṣeṇa prakṛtiḥ sūyate sacarācaram
hetunānena kaunteya jagadviparivartate
verses.essence: Under my supervision, nature brings forth the moving and the unmoving. By this cause, Kaunteya, the world revolves.
verses.translation: Under Me as supervisor, Nature produces the moving and the unmoving; because of this, O Arjuna, the world revolves.

anvaya.meaning: with me as overseer, prakriti
brings forth the moving and unmoving
by this cause, O Kaunteya
the world revolves

verses.explanation: The division of labor stated cleanly: prakṛti does the producing; he presides — adhyakṣeṇa, as the overseeing presence whose mere witness sets the whole factory in motion, the way the sun farms nothing yet ripens everything. Power exercised as presidency, not as labor: the world turns because it is watched.
verses.connection: Completes 9.7–9's cosmology: nature the engine, the Lord its still ignition.
verses.schools_note: 'Mayadhyakshena prakritih suyate' — under My supervision nature produces: the Gita's concise theology of creation. Advaita reads adhyaksha as the witnessing presence whose mere proximity animates; Vishishtadvaita as the inner controller actively willing through nature; Dvaita as efficient cause distinct from matter. All use the verse for the same equilibrium: God neither absent from the machine nor laboring in it.
verses.reflection: Remember once today who supervises the machinery — and let prakriti handle the machinery part.
verses.background: The sovereign paradox is stated: all this world is pervaded by Me; all beings rest in Me — and I am not in them; nor do beings rest in Me. Behold My divine yoga: like wind standing in space, beings stand in Me; nature, under My supervision, produces the moving and unmoving.

verses.devanagari:
अवजानन्ति मां मूढा मानुषीं तनुमाश्रितम् ।
परं भावमजानन्तो मम भूतमहेश्वरम्॥
verses.transliteration:
avajānanti māṃ mūḍhā mānuṣīṃ tanumāśritam
paraṃ bhāvamajānanto mama bhūtamaheśvaram
verses.essence: The deluded disregard me when I dwell in human form — not knowing my higher being as the great Lord of all that is.
verses.translation: Fools disregard Me, clad in human form, not knowing My higher Being as the great Lord of (all) beings.

anvaya.meaning: the deluded disregard me
resorting to a human body
not knowing the higher being
mine — the great Lord of beings

verses.explanation: The cost of the camouflage: avajānanti — they look down on him, the overseer of 9.10, because he stands beside them wearing skin. It is the permanent scandal of incarnation: the infinite made approachable is mistaken for merely ordinary. Familiarity, which should have been the gift, becomes the veil.
verses.connection: The shadow of divine nearness: what 7.24–25 warned about, witnessed on the ground.
verses.reflection: Catch yourself disregarding the divine in human dress today — the ordinary form is the disguise.
verses.background: Krishna names the great mistake and its opposite: fools disregard Me dwelling in human form, not knowing My higher being — while the great souls, sheltered in the divine nature, worship with undistracted mind, ever singing, striving, bowing.

verses.devanagari:
मोघाशा मोघकर्माणो मोघज्ञाना विचेतसः ।
राक्षसीमासुरीं चैव प्रकृतिं मोहिनीं श्रिताः॥
verses.transliteration:
moghāśā moghakarmāṇo moghajñānā vicetasaḥ
rākṣasīmāsurīṃ caiva prakṛtiṃ mohinīṃ śritāḥ
verses.essence: Of vain hopes, vain works, vain knowledge, devoid of sense — they take on the deluding nature of fiends and demons.
verses.translation: Of vain hopes, of vain actions, of vain knowledge and senseless, they verily are possessed of the deceitful nature of demons and undivine beings.

anvaya.meaning: of futile hopes, of futile actions
of futile knowledge, mindless
the fiendish and the demonic
deluding nature, they have embraced

verses.explanation: Where the disregard of 9.11 leads: mogha three times — hopes, works, knowledge, all running on empty, because a life mis-sourced at the root mis-fires in every branch. To miss the divine in the world's center is not one error among many; it is the error that hollows out all the others.
verses.connection: Traces 9.11's contempt to its harvest: a whole existence gone mogha, futile.
verses.reflection: Check your hopes, works, and knowledge for vanity once today — empty things keep busy schedules.
verses.background: Krishna names the great mistake and its opposite: fools disregard Me dwelling in human form, not knowing My higher being — while the great souls, sheltered in the divine nature, worship with undistracted mind, ever singing, striving, bowing.

verses.devanagari:
महात्मानस्तु मां पार्थ दैवीं प्रकृतिमाश्रिताः ।
भजन्त्यनन्यमनसो ज्ञात्वा भूतादिमव्ययम्॥
verses.transliteration:
mahātmānastu māṃ pārtha daivīṃ prakṛtimāśritāḥ
bhajantyananyamanaso jñātvā bhūtādimavyayam
verses.essence: But the great souls, Partha, sheltered in the divine nature, worship me with undistracted mind — knowing me as the imperishable origin of beings.
verses.translation: But the great souls, O Arjuna, partaking of My divine nature, worship Me with a single mind (with the mind devoted to nothing else), knowing Me as the imperishable source of beings.

anvaya.meaning: but the great souls, O Partha, me
sheltered in the divine nature
worship with minds on nothing else
knowing (me) the imperishable source of beings

verses.explanation: The counter-portrait: same human form before them, opposite recognition. The mahātmās see through the skin to the bhūtādi, the origin of all that is, and their response is ananya-manasaḥ — attention without a second address. Greatness of soul, in this chapter's ledger, is measured purely by accuracy of recognition.
verses.connection: The fork from 9.11–12: two kinds of people, distinguished only by what they see in the same place.
verses.reflection: Borrow the mahatma's posture for an hour: sheltered in the divine nature, knowing the Imperishable source.
verses.background: Krishna names the great mistake and its opposite: fools disregard Me dwelling in human form, not knowing My higher being — while the great souls, sheltered in the divine nature, worship with undistracted mind, ever singing, striving, bowing.

verses.devanagari:
सततं कीर्तयन्तो मां यतन्तश्च दृढव्रताः ।
नमस्यन्तश्च मां भक्त्या नित्ययुक्ता उपासते॥
verses.transliteration:
satataṃ kīrtayanto māṃ yatantaśca dṛḍhavratāḥ
namasyantaśca māṃ bhaktyā nityayuktā upāsate
verses.essence: Ever glorifying me and striving with firm vows, bowing to me in devotion — always harnessed, they worship me.
verses.translation: Always glorifying Me, striving,firm in vows, prostrating themselves before Me, they worship Me with devotion always steadfast.

anvaya.meaning: constantly glorifying me
and striving, firm in vows
and bowing to me with devotion
ever-yoked, they worship

verses.explanation: The texture of the great souls' days: kīrtana, the spilling-over of love into song and speech; effort held by vows; the body folded in namas. Note that worship here is continuous tense throughout — satatam, nitya — not an appointment kept but a current lived in. Devotion as climate, not weather.
verses.connection: Shows 9.13's recognition in practice — the daily liturgy of those who see.
verses.reflection: Keep one small vow firmly today and bow once inwardly — perpetual yoking is built of such bricks.
verses.background: Krishna names the great mistake and its opposite: fools disregard Me dwelling in human form, not knowing My higher being — while the great souls, sheltered in the divine nature, worship with undistracted mind, ever singing, striving, bowing.

verses.devanagari:
ज्ञानयज्ञेन चाप्यन्ये यजन्तो मामुपासते ।
एकत्वेन पृथक्त्वेन बहुधा विश्वतोमुखम्॥
verses.transliteration:
jñānayajñena cāpyanye yajanto māmupāsate
ekatvena pṛthaktvena bahudhā viśvatomukham
verses.essence: Others, sacrificing with the sacrifice of knowledge, worship me as the One, as the distinct, as the manifold — me, facing every direction.
verses.translation: Others also sacrificing with the wisdom-sacrifice worship Me, the All-faced, as one, as distinct, and as manifold.

anvaya.meaning: and others, by the sacrifice of knowledge
sacrificing, worship me
as oneness, as separateness
as the manifold — the all-facing

verses.explanation: The philosophers' worship admitted alongside the singers': some adore him as the One without second, some as the Other to be loved across a sacred distance, some as the many faces of the world itself. Viśvato-mukham — facing all ways — he receives every metaphysics as a mode of approach. The map wars are his to smile at; all the maps are of him.
verses.connection: Extends 9.14's devotion to its intellectual forms — monist, dualist, pluralist, all counted as yajna.
verses.reflection: Worship by your own grain today — as one, as distinct, as the all-faced — every geometry is received.
verses.background: Krishna names the great mistake and its opposite: fools disregard Me dwelling in human form, not knowing My higher being — while the great souls, sheltered in the divine nature, worship with undistracted mind, ever singing, striving, bowing.

verses.devanagari:
अहं क्रतुरहं यज्ञः स्वधाहमहमौषधम् ।
मन्त्रोऽहमहमेवाज्यमहमग्निरहं हुतम्॥
verses.transliteration:
ahaṃ kraturahaṃ yajñaḥ svadhāhamahamauṣadham
mantro'hamahamevājyamahamagnirahaṃ hutam
verses.essence: I am the rite, I am the sacrifice, I am the offering to the ancestors, I am the herb; I am the mantra, I the clarified butter, I the fire, I the oblation.
verses.translation: I am the Kratu; I am the Yajna; I am the offering (food) to the manes; I am the medicinal herbs and all the plants; I am the Mantra; I am also the Ghee or the melted butter; I am the fire; I am the oblation.

anvaya.meaning: I am the Vedic rite, I am the sacrifice
I am the ancestral offering, I the herb
I am the mantra, I indeed the ghee
I am the fire, I the act of offering

verses.explanation: The 'I am' litany begins, and its first sweep claims the whole apparatus of religion: the ritual, its words, its butter and flame and the very act of pouring. Worship, analyzed, contains no ingredient that is not him — the worshiped, yes, but also the worship. He is on both sides of every altar.
verses.connection: Opens the great self-declaration (9.16–19), echoing 4.24's Brahman-in-every-element at full voice.
verses.reflection: Find Him in the verbs of your day once: the offering, the fire, the act of offering itself.
verses.background: The voice of the Lord annexes the entire ritual cosmos in first person: I am the rite, the sacrifice, the offering, the herb, the mantra, the fire; father, mother, grandsire of the world; the goal, the witness, the abode, the friend; heat, rain, immortality and death, being and non-being.

verses.devanagari:
पिताहमस्य जगतो माता धाता पितामहः ।
वेद्यं पवित्रमोंकार ऋक्साम यजुरेव च॥
verses.transliteration:
pitāhamasya jagato mātā dhātā pitāmahaḥ
vedyaṃ pavitramoṃkāra ṛksāma yajureva ca
verses.essence: I am the father of this universe, the mother, the sustainer, the grandsire; the object of knowledge, the purifier, the syllable Om; the Rik, the Sama and the Yajus.
verses.translation: I am the father of this world, the mother, the dispenser of the fruits of actions and the grandfather; the (one) thing to be known, the purifier, the sacred monosyllable (Om), and also the Rik-, the Sama-and the Yajur-Vedas.

anvaya.meaning: I am the father of this universe
the mother, the supporter, the grandsire
the to-be-known, the purifier, the Omkara
and the Rik, Sama and Yajus

verses.explanation: From ritual to family: pitā, mātā — and mother is no afterthought; the universe is parented from both sides by the same One. Then lineage stretches back (grandsire) and scripture itself folds in: the three Vedas are not about him; they are him, the way a person's voice is the person.
verses.connection: The litany turns intimate: cosmos as household, with God at every kinship position.
verses.reflection: Address the universe familially once today: father, mother, grandsire — and feel the cosmos warm.
verses.background: The voice of the Lord annexes the entire ritual cosmos in first person: I am the rite, the sacrifice, the offering, the herb, the mantra, the fire; father, mother, grandsire of the world; the goal, the witness, the abode, the friend; heat, rain, immortality and death, being and non-being.

verses.devanagari:
गतिर्भर्ता प्रभुः साक्षी निवासः शरणं सुहृत् ।
प्रभवः प्रलयः स्थानं निधानं बीजमव्ययम्॥
verses.transliteration:
gatirbhartā prabhuḥ sākṣī nivāsaḥ śaraṇaṃ suhṛt
prabhavaḥ pralayaḥ sthānaṃ nidhānaṃ bījamavyayam
verses.essence: I am the goal, the sustainer, the lord, the witness, the abode, the refuge, the friend; the origin, the dissolution, the foundation, the treasury, the imperishable seed.
verses.translation: I am the goal, the supporter, the Lord, the witness, the abode, the shelter, the friend, the origin, the dissolution, the foundation, the treasure-house and the seed which is imperishable.

anvaya.meaning: the goal, the supporter, the lord, the witness
the dwelling, the refuge, the friend
the origin, the dissolution, the ground
the treasury, the imperishable seed

verses.explanation: Twelve names in two lines, and buried among the cosmic titles, one small word glows: suhṛt — friend, the well-wisher who needs no reason (5.29's word returning). The verse sweeps from gatiḥ, the destination of all journeys, to bījam avyayam, the seed never spent — and insists the same One is also simply *on your side*.
verses.connection: Continues 9.16–17's roll-call from functions to relationships — power that is also companionship.
verses.reflection: Pick one of the nine names for your need today — refuge, friend, witness, abode — and use it.
verses.background: The voice of the Lord annexes the entire ritual cosmos in first person: I am the rite, the sacrifice, the offering, the herb, the mantra, the fire; father, mother, grandsire of the world; the goal, the witness, the abode, the friend; heat, rain, immortality and death, being and non-being.

verses.devanagari:
तपाम्यहमहं वर्षं निगृह्णाम्युत्सृजामि च ।
अमृतं चैव मृत्युश्च सदसच्चाहमर्जुन॥
verses.transliteration:
tapāmyahamahaṃ varṣaṃ nigṛhṇāmyutsṛjāmi ca
amṛtaṃ caiva mṛtyuśca sadasaccāhamarjuna
verses.essence: I give heat; I withhold and send forth the rain. I am immortality and also death; being and non-being am I, Arjuna.
verses.translation: (As the sun) I give heat; I withhold and send forth the rain; I am immortality and also death, existence and non-existence, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: I give heat; I, the rain
withhold and release
and immortality, and death
and being and non-being am I, Arjuna

verses.explanation: The litany ends by refusing every flattering edit: not only sun and rain but the withholding of rain; not only amṛta but mṛtyu, death itself; sat *and* asat. A God who owned only the pleasant column would be half a God and no refuge in the dark. This completeness is the chapter's severest comfort: nothing that happens, happens outside him.
verses.connection: Closes the self-declaration at full span — both poles of every pair, claimed.
verses.reflection: Bless both weathers today: the heat and the rain, the holding back and the sending forth.
verses.background: The voice of the Lord annexes the entire ritual cosmos in first person: I am the rite, the sacrifice, the offering, the herb, the mantra, the fire; father, mother, grandsire of the world; the goal, the witness, the abode, the friend; heat, rain, immortality and death, being and non-being.

verses.devanagari:
त्रैविद्या मां सोमपाः पूतपापा
यज्ञैरिष्ट्वा स्वर्गतिं प्रार्थयन्ते ।
ते पुण्यमासाद्य सुरेन्द्रलोक-
मश्नन्ति दिव्यान्दिवि देवभोगान्॥
verses.transliteration:
traividyā māṃ somapāḥ pūtapāpā yajñairiṣṭvā svargatiṃ prārthayante
te puṇyamāsādya surendralokaṃ aśnanti divyāndivi devabhogān
verses.essence: The knowers of the three Vedas, soma-drinkers cleansed of sin, worship me with sacrifice seeking heaven; reaching Indra's holy world, they taste the gods' celestial pleasures.
verses.translation: The knowers of the three Vedas, the drinkers of Soma, purified of all sins, worshipping Me by sacrifices, pray for the way to heaven; they reach the holy world of the Lord of the gods and enjoy in heavn the divine pleasures of the gods.

anvaya.meaning: the three-Veda knowers, soma-drinkers, sin-cleansed
worshiping with sacrifices, pray for the way to heaven
they, attaining the holy world of the gods' lord
enjoy in heaven the divine enjoyments of gods

verses.explanation: The orthodox high-achievers get their due: ritual mastered, sins washed, heaven won — the system works exactly as advertised. The verse withholds all sarcasm; the soma-drinkers truly arrive at Indra's banquet. The audit comes next, and it concerns not the prize's reality but its battery life.
verses.connection: Begins the heaven-economics passage (9.20–21): merit's genuine, finite returns.
verses.reflection: Watch a reward expire today — pleasure ending on schedule — and want the unexpiring a little more.
verses.background: Krishna audits religion itself: the Veda-knowers who drink soma reach heaven and, merit spent, return — coming and going, ruled by desire. But for those who think of Me with no other thought, I carry what they lack and preserve what they have; worshipers reach what they worship — My devotees come to Me.

verses.devanagari:
ते तं भुक्त्वा स्वर्गलोकं विशालं
क्षीणे पुण्ये मर्त्यलोकं विशन्ति ।
एवं त्रयीधर्ममनुप्रपन्ना
गतागतं कामकामा लभन्ते॥
verses.transliteration:
te taṃ bhuktvā svargalokaṃ viśālaṃ kṣīṇe puṇye martyalokaṃ viśanti
evaṃ trayīdharmamanuprapannā gatāgataṃ kāmakāmā labhante
verses.essence: Having enjoyed that vast heaven, their merit exhausted, they re-enter the mortal world. Thus following the law of the three Vedas, desiring desires, they obtain only coming and going.
verses.translation: They, having enjoyed the vast heaven, enter the world of mortals when their merit is exhausted; thus abiding by the injunctions of the ï1threeï1 (Vedas) and desiring (objects of) desires, they attain to the state of going and returning.

anvaya.meaning: they, having enjoyed that vast heaven-world
their merit spent, enter the mortal world
thus following the threefold (Vedic) law
desiring desires, they obtain going-and-coming

verses.explanation: The battery dies: kṣīṇe puṇye — merit, being earned, is spendable, and heaven runs on it like a meter. Then the re-entry, and the chapter's most economical phrase for samsara: gatāgatam, going-and-coming, the commute of souls. Desiring desires (kāma-kāmāḥ), they receive exactly that — desire's infinite installment plan.
verses.connection: The expiry-date on 9.20's prize, setting up the contrast of 9.22's unmetered care.
verses.reflection: Stop commuting between earning and spending merit; ask today for what does not run out.
verses.background: Krishna audits religion itself: the Veda-knowers who drink soma reach heaven and, merit spent, return — coming and going, ruled by desire. But for those who think of Me with no other thought, I carry what they lack and preserve what they have; worshipers reach what they worship — My devotees come to Me.

verses.devanagari:
अनन्याश्चिन्तयन्तो मां ये जनाः पर्युपासते ।
तेषां नित्याभियुक्तानां योगक्षेमं वहाम्यहम्॥
verses.transliteration:
ananyāścintayanto māṃ ye janāḥ paryupāsate
teṣāṃ nityābhiyuktānāṃ yogakṣemaṃ vahāmyaham
verses.essence: For those who worship me thinking of nothing else, ever harnessed — I carry what they lack, and I preserve what they have.
verses.translation: For those men who worship Me alone, thinking of no other, for those ever-united, I secure what is not already possessed and preserve what they already possess.

anvaya.meaning: thinking (of me) with no other (object)
the people who worship all around
of them, the ever-steadfast
the acquiring and the keeping — I carry

verses.explanation: The chapter's most cherished sentence, hinged on one verb: vahāmi, *I carry* — not 'arrange', not 'grant': shoulder, personally, like a porter. Yoga is the obtaining of what is lacking, kṣema the guarding of what is held; together they are the entire economy of anxiety — and he takes over both departments for whoever stops dividing their attention. The contract has one clause: ananyāḥ, no second object. Ananyash chintayanto mam — the yoga-kshema verse, the one devotional households paint on their walls: for those who think of Me with no second thought, I carry what they lack and guard what they have. Vahami — I bear, as a porter bears: Ramanuja reportedly trembled at teaching it, that the Lord makes Himself the devotee's servant of logistics. It is the Gita's most audacious promise of providence.
verses.connection: The answer to 9.21's metered heaven: undivided devotion is the one account he funds himself.
verses.schools_note: A verse households have lived on for centuries. Tradition tells of a poor brahmin who doubted the word 'carry,' tried to scratch it out — and found his family fed by a stranger bearing wounds where the scratch fell. Commentators stress the audacity: God as the devotee's provider and guard, reversing every usual direction of service.
verses.reflection: Give Him your undivided hour and let Him carry the logistics — yoga-kshemam vahamy aham. Test it.
verses.background: Krishna audits religion itself: the Veda-knowers who drink soma reach heaven and, merit spent, return — coming and going, ruled by desire. But for those who think of Me with no other thought, I carry what they lack and preserve what they have; worshipers reach what they worship — My devotees come to Me.

verses.devanagari:
येऽप्यन्यदेवता भक्ता यजन्ते श्रद्धयान्विताः ।
तेऽपि मामेव कौन्तेय यजन्त्यविधिपूर्वकम्॥
verses.transliteration:
ye'pyanyadevatābhaktā yajante śraddhayānvitāḥ
te'pi māmeva kaunteya yajantyavidhipūrvakam
verses.essence: Even those devoted to other gods, who sacrifice to them filled with faith — they too worship me alone, Kaunteya, though not according to the rule.
verses.translation: Even those devotees who, endowed with faith, worship other gods, worship Me alone, O Arjuna, b the wrong method.

anvaya.meaning: even those devoted to other deities
who sacrifice, endowed with faith
they too (worship) me alone, O Kaunteya
they sacrifice — (but) not by the rule

verses.explanation: The widest net in the chapter: every faithful offering at every altar lands, in fact, on his table — te 'pi mām eva. The qualifier avidhi-pūrvakam, 'not by the proper rule,' is gentle: not wrong worship but worship with an incomplete address, a letter that arrives because the postmaster knows for whom it was really meant.
verses.connection: Reaffirms 7.21–22 inside the royal teaching: all faith routes to one receiver.
verses.reflection: Honor every sincere worship you see today — it reaches Him, though the address be approximate.
verses.background: Krishna audits religion itself: the Veda-knowers who drink soma reach heaven and, merit spent, return — coming and going, ruled by desire. But for those who think of Me with no other thought, I carry what they lack and preserve what they have; worshipers reach what they worship — My devotees come to Me.

verses.devanagari:
अहं हि सर्वयज्ञानां भोक्ता च प्रभुरेव च ।
न तु मामभिजानन्ति तत्त्वेनातश्च्यवन्ति ते॥
verses.transliteration:
ahaṃ hi sarvayajñānāṃ bhoktā ca prabhureva ca
na tu māmabhijānanti tattvenātaścyavanti te
verses.essence: For I am the enjoyer and the lord of all sacrifices. But they do not know me in truth — and so they fall back.
verses.translation: (For) I alone am the enjoyer and also the Lord of all sacrifices; but they do not know Me in essence (in reality), and hence they fall (return to this mortal world).

anvaya.meaning: for I, of all sacrifices
am the enjoyer and the lord indeed
but they do not recognize me
in truth — therefore they fall

verses.explanation: Why the incomplete address matters: the offering arrives, but the offerer's *aim* terminates at the intermediary — and one travels to one's aim (8.6's law). Cyavanti, they fall back: not punished, simply returned from a finite destination. The loss is not of the gift but of the giver's own trajectory.
verses.connection: Completes 9.23: received by him, yes — but the worshiper journeys only as far as they knowingly sent themselves.
verses.reflection: Aim the offering correctly once today: to the Enjoyer of all sacrifice, by name.
verses.background: Krishna audits religion itself: the Veda-knowers who drink soma reach heaven and, merit spent, return — coming and going, ruled by desire. But for those who think of Me with no other thought, I carry what they lack and preserve what they have; worshipers reach what they worship — My devotees come to Me.

verses.devanagari:
यान्ति देवव्रता देवान्पितॄन्यान्ति पितृव्रताः ।
भूतानि यान्ति भूतेज्या यान्ति मद्याजिनोऽपि माम्॥
verses.transliteration:
yānti devavratā devānpitṝnyānti pitṛvratāḥ
bhūtāni yānti bhūtejyā yānti madyājino'pi mām
verses.essence: Votaries of the gods go to the gods; votaries of the ancestors go to the ancestors; worshipers of spirits go to the spirits; and those who sacrifice to me come to me.
verses.translation: The worshippers of the gods go to them; to the manes go the ancestor-worshippers; to the deities who preside over the elements go their worshippers; but My devotees come to Me.

anvaya.meaning: god-vowed go to the gods
to the ancestors go the ancestor-vowed
to the spirits go the spirit-worshipers
and my sacrificers come to me

verses.explanation: The law of 9.24 written as a fare table, four lines, no exceptions: destination equals devotion. Each row is honored — the gods' votaries truly reach the gods — and each fare costs the same coin: a life's orientation. The last line's quiet asymmetry is the chapter's whole argument: for identical fare, one route ends where there is no further terminus.
verses.connection: The summary timetable of 9.20–24: every aim arrives — so aim at the unsurpassable.
verses.reflection: Notice the law of destinations: you arrive at what you worship. Check your itinerary tonight.
verses.background: Krishna audits religion itself: the Veda-knowers who drink soma reach heaven and, merit spent, return — coming and going, ruled by desire. But for those who think of Me with no other thought, I carry what they lack and preserve what they have; worshipers reach what they worship — My devotees come to Me.

verses.devanagari:
पत्रं पुष्पं फलं तोयं यो मे भक्त्या प्रयच्छति ।
तदहं भक्त्युपहृतमश्नामि प्रयतात्मनः॥
verses.transliteration:
patraṃ puṣpaṃ phalaṃ toyaṃ yo me bhaktyā prayacchati
tadahaṃ bhaktyupahṛtamaśnāmi prayatātmanaḥ
verses.essence: A leaf, a flower, a fruit, water — whoever offers these to me with devotion, that offering of love from a striving soul, I accept.
verses.translation: Whoever offers Me with devotion a leaf, a flower, a fruit or a little water that, so offered devotedly by the pure-minded, I accept.

anvaya.meaning: a leaf, a flower, a fruit, water
who offers to me with devotion
that, brought-near by devotion, I
accept and enjoy — of the pure-striving soul

verses.explanation: From cosmic fare-tables to a leaf in an open palm. The price of reaching the unsurpassable is set at whatever the poorest hand can hold — provided it carries bhakti. Aśnāmi: he does not merely accept but *partakes*, eats, as a parent eats a child's offered crumb. The economy of 9.22 in miniature: love is the only currency, and the smallest denomination clears. Patram pushpam phalam toyam — a leaf, a flower, a fruit, water: the minimalist's charter, the verse that made the poorest offering sufficient forever. The operative clause is bhaktya prayatatmanah — offered with love, by a striving self: tradition reads tad aham ashnami, 'I eat it,' with full literalness in temple practice. The economy of grace runs on sincerity, not substance.
verses.connection: The most quoted verse of practical devotion: the door of 9.25's highest destination, shown to be unlocked.
verses.schools_note: The leaf-flower-fruit-water verse is bhakti's legal minimum, and the schools annotate the operative word: 'bhaktya' — Ramanuja notes the Lord accepts not the item but the love riding it; Shankara that prayatatmanah (the striving, purified self) names the real offering. Temple practice took 'ashnami' — I eat it — literally: the entire institution of naivedya stands on this verse.
verses.reflection: Offer the smallest thing today — a leaf, a sip of water, a minute — with actual love. It is enough.
verses.background: The chapter ends in the widest opening of the poem: a leaf, a flower, fruit, water offered with love is accepted; make every act an offering; I am equal to all, yet My lovers are in Me; even the worst, rightly resolved, swiftly turns righteous; no one devoted to Me is ever lost — whatever their birth. Then the fourfold instruction: mind on Me, devoted, sacrificing, bowing.

verses.devanagari:
यत्करोषि यदश्नासि यज्जुहोषि ददासि यत् ।
यत्तपस्यसि कौन्तेय तत्कुरुष्व मदर्पणम्॥
verses.transliteration:
yatkaroṣi yadaśnāsi yajjuhoṣi dadāsi yat
yattapasyasi kaunteya tatkuruṣva madarpaṇam
verses.essence: Whatever you do, whatever you eat, whatever you offer, whatever you give, whatever austerity you perform, Kaunteya — do it as an offering to me.
verses.translation: Whatever thou doest, whatever thou eatest, whatever thou offerest in sacrifice, whatever thou givest, whatever thou practisest as austerity, O Arjuna, do it as an offering unto Me.

anvaya.meaning: whatever you do, whatever you eat
whatever you offer, whatever you give
whatever austerity you perform, O Kaunteya
do that as an offering to me

verses.explanation: The leaf-and-water principle expanded to the whole of life: not new acts added, but every existing act re-addressed — mad-arpaṇam, made over to me. Cooking, eating, working, giving: the day's full inventory becomes liturgy without changing a single item on it. This is the karma-yoga of chapter 3, finally given its recipient by name. Yat karoshi yad ashnasi — whatever you do, eat, offer, give, whatever austerity you perform — make it an offering to Me: the verse that converted the entire day into liturgy. Commentators call it the universal version of 9.26: where that verse blessed the smallest gift, this one annexes the whole calendar. Arpanam as a way of life: the kitchen, the desk, the road — all altar.
verses.connection: Universalizes 9.26: from one devotional offering to existence itself as the offering.
verses.reflection: Convert the day wholesale: whatever you do, eat, give, undergo — append silently, 'this is offered.'
verses.background: The chapter ends in the widest opening of the poem: a leaf, a flower, fruit, water offered with love is accepted; make every act an offering; I am equal to all, yet My lovers are in Me; even the worst, rightly resolved, swiftly turns righteous; no one devoted to Me is ever lost — whatever their birth. Then the fourfold instruction: mind on Me, devoted, sacrificing, bowing.

verses.devanagari:
शुभाशुभफलैरेवं मोक्ष्यसे कर्मबन्धनैः ।
संन्यासयोगयुक्तात्मा विमुक्तो मामुपैष्यसि॥
verses.transliteration:
śubhāśubhaphalairevaṃ mokṣyase karmabandhanaiḥ
saṃnyāsayogayuktātmā vimukto māmupaiṣyasi
verses.essence: Thus you will be freed from the bonds of action with their fair and foul fruits; with self harnessed by the yoga of renunciation, liberated, you will come to me.
verses.translation: Thus shalt thou be freed from the bonds of actions yielding good and evil fruits; with the mind steadfast in the Yoga of renunciation, and liberated, thou shalt come unto Me.

anvaya.meaning: from the fair-and-foul-fruited, thus
you will be freed — bonds of action
with self yoked by the yoga of renunciation
fully liberated, you will come to me

verses.explanation: The mechanism stated: what is given away can no longer bind the giver. Actions offered in 9.27 stop depositing their fruits — fair or foul — in the doer's account; the ledger simply closes. Sannyāsa-yoga: renunciation not as leaving acts but as leaving ownership of them, the chapter's working synthesis of all earlier paths.
verses.connection: The fruit of 9.27's re-addressing: karma's bookkeeping ends, and the freed account-holder comes home.
verses.reflection: Walk free of one result today, good or bad, by having offered the act before doing it.
verses.background: The chapter ends in the widest opening of the poem: a leaf, a flower, fruit, water offered with love is accepted; make every act an offering; I am equal to all, yet My lovers are in Me; even the worst, rightly resolved, swiftly turns righteous; no one devoted to Me is ever lost — whatever their birth. Then the fourfold instruction: mind on Me, devoted, sacrificing, bowing.

verses.devanagari:
समोऽहं सर्वभूतेषु न मे द्वेष्योऽस्ति न प्रियः ।
ये भजन्ति तु मां भक्त्या मयि ते तेषु चाप्यहम्॥
verses.transliteration:
samo'haṃ sarvabhūteṣu na me dveṣyo'sti na priyaḥ
ye bhajanti tu māṃ bhaktyā mayi te teṣu cāpyaham
verses.essence: I am the same toward all beings; none is hateful to me, none dear. But those who worship me with devotion — they are in me, and I too am in them.
verses.translation: The same am I to all beings; to Me there is none hateful or dear; but those who worship Me with devotion are in Me and I am also in them.

anvaya.meaning: equal am I toward all beings

anvaya.word: न मे द्वेष्यः अस्ति न प्रियः
anvaya.meaning: none is hateful to me, none dear
but those who worship me with devotion

anvaya.word: मयि ते तेषु च अपि अहम्
anvaya.meaning: they are in me, and I too in them

verses.explanation: Two truths that look like rivals, held in one breath: perfect impartiality — the sun owing no one extra light — and yet a real, mutual indwelling with those who love him. The resolution is in who moves: his sameness never wavers; the devotee steps into the always-shining. Grace is not favoritism; it is exposure. Samo 'ham sarva-bhuteshu — I am equal to all beings, none hated, none favored — and then the warmth inside the impartiality: but those who love Me are in Me, and I in them. The commentaries resolve the seeming tension with the sun image: shining equally, yet entering the opened window. Divine impartiality is the constancy; intimacy is what reciprocity does with it.
verses.connection: Reconciles the chapter's universal God with its intimate one — equality below, communion above.
verses.schools_note: Commentators savor the tension: Shankara reads the indwelling as the fire glowing where fuel is offered, no partiality in the fire; Ramanuja dares further — that the Lord, by his own word, comes to depend on his devotees as they on him. The verse supports both: even-handed, and yet entered.
verses.reflection: Trust the impartial sun today: equally on all, and intimate with whoever steps outside.
verses.background: The chapter ends in the widest opening of the poem: a leaf, a flower, fruit, water offered with love is accepted; make every act an offering; I am equal to all, yet My lovers are in Me; even the worst, rightly resolved, swiftly turns righteous; no one devoted to Me is ever lost — whatever their birth. Then the fourfold instruction: mind on Me, devoted, sacrificing, bowing.

verses.devanagari:
अपि चेत्सुदुराचारो भजते मामनन्यभाक् ।
साधुरेव स मन्तव्यः सम्यग्व्यवसितो हि सः॥
verses.transliteration:
api cetsudurācāro bhajate māmananyabhāk
sādhureva sa mantavyaḥ samyagvyavasito hi saḥ
verses.essence: Even if one of very evil conduct worships me with undivided devotion, that one must be reckoned righteous — for the resolve is rightly set.
verses.translation: Even if the most sinful worships Me, with devotion to none else, he too should indeed by regarded as righteous for he has rightly resolved.

anvaya.meaning: even if one of very bad conduct
worships me, sharing with no other
that one is to be considered righteous indeed
for rightly resolved is he

verses.explanation: The most generous sentence in the scripture's most generous chapter: take the worst résumé — su-durācāraḥ — and one undivided turning outweighs it, mantavyaḥ, *must* be so reckoned, an instruction to the judging world as much as a comfort to the sinner. The logic: conduct is the past; vyavasāya, the set of the will, is the trajectory — and persons are their trajectories.
verses.connection: The chapter's amnesty: 9.26's open door, confirmed open even to the disgraced.
verses.schools_note: 'Api chet su-duracharah' — even the very ill-conducted, once turned with undivided devotion, 'is to be counted righteous, for he has rightly resolved.' Commentators guard the verse from license: Shankara and Ramanuja both read 9.31's 'kshipram bhavati dharmatma' — he swiftly becomes righteous — as the proof: true bhakti reforms conduct; it does not excuse it. The verse's scandal and its safety are the same fact: resolve re-titles a past.
verses.reflection: Refuse to finalize anyone today — including yourself. One true resolve re-titles a whole past.
verses.background: The chapter ends in the widest opening of the poem: a leaf, a flower, fruit, water offered with love is accepted; make every act an offering; I am equal to all, yet My lovers are in Me; even the worst, rightly resolved, swiftly turns righteous; no one devoted to Me is ever lost — whatever their birth. Then the fourfold instruction: mind on Me, devoted, sacrificing, bowing.

verses.devanagari:
क्षिप्रं भवति धर्मात्मा शश्वच्छान्तिं निगच्छति ।
कौन्तेय प्रतिजानीहि न मे भक्तः प्रणश्यति॥
verses.transliteration:
kṣipraṃ bhavati dharmātmā śaśvacchāntiṃ nigacchati
kaunteya pratijānīhi na me bhaktaḥ praṇaśyati
verses.essence: Swiftly such a one becomes a soul of dharma and attains lasting peace. Kaunteya, declare it: my devotee never perishes.
verses.translation: Soon he becomes righteous and attains to eternal peace; O Arjuna, proclaim thou for certain that My devotee never perishes.

anvaya.meaning: swiftly one becomes a dharma-souled one
and attains everlasting peace
O Kaunteya, declare, promise
my devotee does not perish

verses.explanation: The promise of 9.30 secured, with a startling delegation: pratijānīhi — *you* proclaim it, Arjuna. Tradition hears tenderness in the maneuver: the Lord's own vows have, in the epics, bent for love's sake; a promise placed in the devotee's mouth he will never allow to break. Na me bhaktaḥ praṇaśyati: four words generations have gripped in the dark.
verses.connection: Seals 9.30's amnesty into oath — and makes Arjuna its guarantor.
verses.reflection: Say the guarantee to someone who needs it, or to yourself: My devotee is never lost.
verses.background: The chapter ends in the widest opening of the poem: a leaf, a flower, fruit, water offered with love is accepted; make every act an offering; I am equal to all, yet My lovers are in Me; even the worst, rightly resolved, swiftly turns righteous; no one devoted to Me is ever lost — whatever their birth. Then the fourfold instruction: mind on Me, devoted, sacrificing, bowing.

verses.devanagari:
मां हि पार्थ व्यपाश्रित्य येऽपि स्युः पापयोनयः ।
स्त्रियो वैश्यास्तथा शूद्रास्तेऽपि यान्ति परां गतिम्॥
verses.transliteration:
māṃ hi pārtha vyapāśritya ye'pi syuḥ pāpayonayaḥ
striyo vaiśyāstathā śūdrāste'pi yānti parāṃ gatim
verses.essence: For those who take refuge in me, Partha — whatever their birth, women, merchants, even laborers — they too reach the supreme goal.
verses.translation: For, taking refuge in Me, they also who, O Arjuna, may be of a sinful birth women, Vaisyas as well as Sudras attain the Supreme Goal.

anvaya.meaning: for, taking full refuge in me, O Partha
even those who may be of stigmatized birth
women, vaishyas, likewise shudras
they too go to the supreme goal

verses.explanation: Read against its era, this verse is a door blown open: every group its society's ritual system sidelined from the highest pursuits — listed here in the period's own blunt categories — is declared eligible, te 'pi yānti parāṁ gatim, for the very summit. The path of refuge asks no pedigree, gender, or caste; it asks only the turning. Later devotional movements built their universalism on exactly this foundation. Mam hi partha vyapashritya — for whoever takes refuge in Me, the verse declares, the supreme goal stands open regardless of birth, station, or past — the list it gives was, in its age, a radical opening of doors the society kept shut. The bhakti movements of every century have read this verse as their charter: the lineage of saints from every excluded origin is its living commentary.
verses.connection: Extends 9.30–31's amnesty across the social order: the royal road is public, in the fullest sense.
verses.schools_note: Commentators across eras read this verse's sweep — refuge open regardless of birth or past — as the Gita's deliberate widening of a door its society kept narrow. Classical glosses retain the period's categories even while affirming the promise; the bhakti movements and modern teachers (Vivekananda, Gandhi, Ambedkar's interlocutors) pressed the verse's own logic: if the supreme goal ignores pedigree, no human arrangement may invoke heaven for exclusion.
verses.reflection: Wherever you started from, the summit is open — let no biography disqualify you from refuge today.
verses.background: The chapter ends in the widest opening of the poem: a leaf, a flower, fruit, water offered with love is accepted; make every act an offering; I am equal to all, yet My lovers are in Me; even the worst, rightly resolved, swiftly turns righteous; no one devoted to Me is ever lost — whatever their birth. Then the fourfold instruction: mind on Me, devoted, sacrificing, bowing.

verses.devanagari:
किं पुनर्ब्राह्मणाः पुण्या भक्ता राजर्षयस्तथा ।
अनित्यमसुखं लोकमिमं प्राप्य भजस्व माम्॥
verses.transliteration:
kiṃ punarbrāhmaṇāḥ puṇyā bhaktā rājarṣayastathā
anityamasukhaṃ lokamimaṃ prāpya bhajasva mām
verses.essence: How much more, then, the pure brahmins and devoted royal sages! Having come to this impermanent, joyless world — worship me.
verses.translation: How much more (easily) then the hold Brahmins and devoted royal saints (attain the goal); having come to this impermanent and unhappy world, do thou worship Me.

anvaya.meaning: how much more the pure brahmins
and the devoted royal sages
this impermanent, joyless world
having attained — worship me

verses.explanation: The a-fortiori turn: if the door stands open to the excluded, those born holding every advantage have least excuse to linger outside it. Then the chapter's bluntest line about the venue: anityam asukham — impermanent and, on net, not happy. Not despair; a clear-eyed travel advisory, with the one worthwhile activity here named in two words: bhajasva mām.
verses.connection: Turns 9.32's universal access into universal urgency: whoever you are, the world won't hold — worship.
verses.reflection: You hold an unfleeting chance in a fleeting world — spend one deliberate hour of it on the highest.
verses.background: The chapter ends in the widest opening of the poem: a leaf, a flower, fruit, water offered with love is accepted; make every act an offering; I am equal to all, yet My lovers are in Me; even the worst, rightly resolved, swiftly turns righteous; no one devoted to Me is ever lost — whatever their birth. Then the fourfold instruction: mind on Me, devoted, sacrificing, bowing.

verses.devanagari:
मन्मना भव मद्भक्तो मद्याजी मां नमस्कुरु ।
मामेवैष्यसि युक्त्वैवमात्मानं मत्परायणः॥
verses.transliteration:
manmanā bhava madbhakto madyājī māṃ namaskuru
māmevaiṣyasi yuktvaivamātmānaṃ matparāyaṇaḥ
verses.essence: Fix your mind on me, be my devotee, sacrifice to me, bow to me. Thus harnessing yourself, with me as the supreme goal, you will come to me.
verses.translation: Fix thy mind on Me; by devoted to Me; sacrifice unto Me; bow down to Me; having thus united thy whole self to Me, taking Me as the supreme goal, thou shalt come unto Me.

anvaya.meaning: be one whose mind is mine, my devotee
my sacrificer; to me bow
to me alone you will come, yoking thus
yourself, with me as the final resort

verses.explanation: The royal secret reduced to four imperatives small enough to carry into battle: mind here, heart here, acts here, body bowed here. Thought, love, work, humility — the four limbs of a life, all given one direction. So central is this formula that the Gita will repeat it nearly verbatim at its own climax (18.65), as if to say: if everything else is forgotten, keep this. Man-mana bhava mad-bhakto — the four-fold instruction the Gita will repeat verbatim at 18.65 as its near-final word: mind on Me, devotion to Me, sacrifice to Me, homage to Me. Commentators note the repetition as the poem's own emphasis-marking — of seven hundred verses, this is the one it says twice. Mat-parayanah: with Me as the way and the destination both.
verses.connection: The chapter's seal — and the seed of the scripture's final instruction nine chapters later.
verses.reflection: Do the fourfold once before sleep: mind on Him, heart to Him, an offering, a bow.
verses.background: The chapter ends in the widest opening of the poem: a leaf, a flower, fruit, water offered with love is accepted; make every act an offering; I am equal to all, yet My lovers are in Me; even the worst, rightly resolved, swiftly turns righteous; no one devoted to Me is ever lost — whatever their birth. Then the fourfold instruction: mind on Me, devoted, sacrificing, bowing.
